did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for automated skin cancer classification (benign vs. malignant) using 3D Total Body Photography (3D-TBP) images. The method includes watershed segmentation for preprocessing, data augmentation, and training with the AdamW optimizer and OneCycleLR scheduler to achieve a classification accuracy of 74.64%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for skin cancer classification using 3D-TBP images. It details specific methodology (watershed segmentation, data augmentation, AdamW optimizer), reports original results (74.64% accuracy, 96.67% specificity), and includes a confusion matrix and performance metrics. Although published in conference proceedings (ICISS-2025), it contains new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection in 3-Dimensional Total Body Photography (3D-TBP) using Deep Learning
journal: Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS-2025)
brief_summary: This study presents an automated skin cancer detection system using a Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify lesions as benign or malignant from 3D Total Body Photography (3D-TBP) images. The methodology involves preprocessing high-resolution images with watershed segmentation for lesion isolation, resizing to 128x128 pixels, and data augmentation to improve generalization. The model was trained using the AdamW optimizer and OneCycleLR scheduler on a diverse dataset containing patient demographics and lesion metadata. The system achieved an overall classification accuracy of 74.64% with high specificity for malignant cases, though sensitivity was noted as a limitation due to false negatives. Results were deployed via a Streamlit-based web application to provide real-time classifications and confidence scores for clinical and preliminary diagnostic use. The authors conclude that while the approach offers a scalable diagnostic tool, future work must address dataset imbalance and improve sensitivity through enhanced data diversity and advanced augmentation strategies.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as benign in the binary classification task; the model achieved 96.67% specificity for identifying these lesions.
Classified as malignant in the binary classification task; the model achieved 18.64% sensitivity for these lesions, with a noted issue of false negatives.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes an automated skin cancer classification system using a Custom CNN to classify lesions as benign or malignant. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant) with reported metrics including accuracy (74.64%), specificity (96.67%), sensitivity (18.64%), and precision (68.75%). Class probabilities are provided as outputs for class prediction (e.g., Benign 78.4%, Malignant 21.6%), which confirms the task is classification, not regression.

datasets_used.name: High-resolution skin cancer dataset (3D-TBP)
datasets_used.description: A private or proprietary dataset containing high-resolution 3D Total Body Photography (3D-TBP) images of skin lesions. The dataset includes metadata on patient demographics, lesion size, and anatomical site. It is described as having a diverse representation of cases but suffering from class imbalance, particularly a lack of diversity in malignant samples.

use.training: Used for training the Custom CNN model for 20 epochs with data augmentation (flips, rotations, brightness/contrast adjustments, hue/saturation shifts, elastic deformations, gamma correction).
use.validation: Used for evaluating model performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1 score) at the end of each epoch.
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as a separate held-out test set; the paper notes a 'small gap between training vs validation' and discusses confusion matrix analysis, implying validation metrics were used for final assessment.
use.other: Used to demonstrate the system's capability to handle real-world skin cancer detection challenges, including preprocessing (resizing to 128x128, normalization, watershed segmentation) and deployment in a Streamlit web application.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize classification results, break down true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives, and understand the distribution of misclassifications.
To monitor model convergence and performance over 20 epochs by analyzing training and validation loss and accuracy graphs.
To evaluate the model's diagnostic capability using sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1 score, and overall accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 score, along with confusion matrix analysis.

High-resolution skin cancer dataset (3D-.notes: The model was trained for 20 epochs using AdamW optimizer and OneCycleLR scheduler. The confusion matrix analysis indicated a higher rate of false negatives (malignant lesions mistakenly classified as benign). A sample prediction showed class probabilities of Benign 78.4% and Malignant 21.6%. The paper notes a small gap between training and validation metrics, suggesting potential for increased regularization or additional data augmentation.

regularization: Data augmentation (horizontal/vertical flips, rotations, brightness/contrast adjustments, hue/saturation shifts, elastic deformations, random gamma correction)

conclusions: The proposed system, integrating a Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with 3D Total Body Photography (3D-TBP) and watershed segmentation, provides a robust and automated approach for skin cancer detection.
The model achieved an overall accuracy of 74.64% and high specificity (96.67%), indicating strong capability in accurately identifying benign lesions.
The model exhibited low sensitivity (18.64%) and a high rate of false negatives, where malignant lesions were mistakenly classified as benign, which has intense clinical consequences.
The low sensitivity is attributed to class imbalance and a lack of diversity in the malignant samples within the dataset.
There is a small gap between training and validation metrics, indicating potential for overfitting and suggesting the need for increased regularization or additional dataset augmentation.
Future work must prioritize improving sensitivity through better data diversity, class balancing, and advanced augmentation strategies to enhance generalization for identifying malignant cases.
Incorporating other imaging modalities, such as hyperspectral or thermal imaging, is recommended to increase diagnostic performance across a wider spectrum of patient populations.
The system was deployed via a Streamlit-based web application, providing an accessible, user-friendly interface for preliminary diagnostics in both clinical and non-clinical settings.
important_remarks: The model exhibits critically low sensitivity (18.64%) with a high rate of false negatives, where malignant lesions are mistakenly classified as benign, posing intense clinical consequences.
Performance is limited by class imbalance and a lack of diversity in malignant samples within the dataset.
A small gap between training and validation metrics suggests potential overfitting, indicating a need for increased regularization or additional data augmentation.
The study relies on a private/proprietary high-resolution 3D-TBP dataset; specific sample counts for training, validation, and testing splits are not reported.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated only on the internal dataset.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the reported metrics.
The system is deployed as a Streamlit web application for preliminary diagnostics, but its clinical utility is constrained by the low sensitivity for malignant cases.
The authors acknowledge the need for future work to address dataset imbalance and incorporate other imaging modalities (e.g., hyperspectral or thermal) to improve diagnostic performance.
